Regulation Name: Medicare Program; Hospital Inpatient Prospective Payment Systems (IPPS) and Long-Term Care Hospital Prospective Payment System FY 2026 Rule
Summary: Updates Medicare hospital inpatient (IPPS) and long‑term care hospital (LTCH) PPS payment policies and rates for FY 2026; adjusts quality reporting and value‑based programs; and finalizes several policy clarifications and technical changes. Includes provisions affecting graduate medical education and hospital quality measures.
Healthcare Implications: Impacts hospital reimbursement, quality reporting, and documentation practices that interact with EHRs and AI‑enabled analytics. Changes to measure sets and program requirements shape how hospitals deploy clinical decision support and reporting automations. Budget and compliance teams should align AI/analytics workflows with updated payment and quality policies.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers; Developers & Vendors

Regulation Name: Medicare and Medicaid Programs; Contract Year 2026 Policy and Technical Changes to Medicare Advantage, Part D, Cost Plans, and PACE
Summary: Finalizes Contract Year 2026 policy and technical changes for Medicare Advantage (Part C), Part D, cost plans, and PACE, including adjustments to the Medicare Prescription Payment Plan, D‑SNPs, Star Ratings, utilization management, and other programmatic areas. Implements IRA‑related changes and clarifies several beneficiary protections.
Healthcare Implications: Affects plan operations, prior authorization, and beneficiary communications–areas where plans increasingly use AI for triage, utilization review, and member outreach. Transparency and documentation requirements inform how AI‑assisted workflows must be governed. Providers may experience updated PA processes and data exchange with MA/Part D plans.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers

Regulation Name: Medicare and Medicaid Programs and CHIP; Hospital Inpatient Prospective Payment Systems for Acute Care Hospitals and LTCH PPS and FY 2025 Rates; Quality Programs Requirements
Summary: Revises hospital inpatient (IPPS) and LTCH PPS payment rates and policies for FY 2025, updates quality and reporting programs, and makes related technical changes. Includes adjustments to graduate medical education policies and clarifications across inpatient hospital payment provisions.
Healthcare Implications: Updates to quality reporting and documentation indirectly shape requirements for EHR configuration, coding, and analytics. Hospitals using AI for documentation integrity, quality tracking, or measure abstraction should confirm alignment with the updated policies. Finance and compliance teams should assess downstream impacts on revenue cycle tools.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers

Regulation Name: Nondiscrimination in Health Programs and Activities
Summary: Finalizes CY 2024 updates to the Physician Fee Schedule and other Medicare Part B policies, including coding/payment updates, changes to the Quality Payment Program, and clarifications for telehealth and behavioral health services.
Healthcare Implications: Updates affect clinician reimbursement and reporting that interact with EHR templates, clinical decision support, and AI‑assisted documentation. Telehealth and behavioral health provisions influence digital tools used in outpatient care and remote monitoring programs.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers; Regulators & Government

Regulation Name: Medicare Program; Changes to MA and Part D for CY 2024 & 2025
Summary: Medicare hospital outpatient (OPPS) and ambulatory surgical center (ASC) payment policies for the specified year, including packaging, device pass‑throughs, and quality reporting updates. Also includes technical corrections and clarifications.
Healthcare Implications: Outpatient payment and reporting changes impact how hospitals and ASCs configure EHR charge capture, quality dashboards, and any AI‑assisted coding or utilization management tools. Device and pass‑through updates may affect adoption pathways for AI‑enabled devices and diagnostics.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers

Regulation Name: Medicare and Medicaid Programs; Advancing Interoperability and Improving Prior Authorization Processes
Summary: Improves electronic exchange of health data and streamlines prior authorization by requiring APIs (e.g., Prior Authorization Support, Patient Access, Payer‑to‑Payer), shorter PA decision timeframes, and new metrics reporting for impacted payers.
Healthcare Implications: Directly affects AI‑assisted prior authorization and care coordination. Payers and providers must integrate API‑based PA workflows, which enable automation and analytics while preserving transparency, timelines, and patient access.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers

Regulation Name: Medicare Program: Hospital Outpatient Prospective Payment and ASC Payment Systems; Payment for Intensive Outpatient Services and Other Policies
Summary: Finalizes CY 2025 updates to the Physician Fee Schedule and other Medicare Part B policies, including relative value unit updates, coverage/payment clarifications, and Quality Payment Program changes.
Healthcare Implications: Impacts clinician billing and reporting systems that often embed AI‑assisted coding, documentation, and quality tracking. Outpatient digital health and remote monitoring programs should validate any code/payment changes.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Payers & Purchasers

Regulation Name: Medicare and Medicaid Programs; CY 2024 Physician Fee Schedule and Other Policy Changes
Summary: Companion entry related to CY 2024 Physician Fee Schedule updates and associated Part B policy changes, including QPP updates and telehealth provisions.
Healthcare Implications: Similar implications for EHR configurations, AI‑assisted documentation, and quality/telehealth workflows in outpatient settings.
Stakeholder Tags: Payers & Purchasers; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Regulation Name: Medicare Program: Hospital Outpatient Prospective Payment and ASC Payment Systems and Quality Reporting Programs; Organ Acquisition; Rural Emergency Hospitals and Other Policies
Summary: Finalizes CY 2023 updates to the Physician Fee Schedule and other Medicare Part B policies, including valuation changes, telehealth flexibilities, and QPP updates.
Healthcare Implications: Continues telehealth and digital health coverage pathways that underpin AI‑enabled remote care and documentation tools. Clinician groups should align AI‑assisted workflows with QPP and coding changes.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers

Regulation Name: Medicare and Medicaid Programs; CY 2023 Physician Fee Schedule and Other Part B Policies
Summary: Updates OPPS and ASC payment policies for CY 2023, including packaging policies, device pass‑throughs, and outpatient quality reporting changes.
Healthcare Implications: Outpatient coding and quality reporting are touchpoints for AI‑assisted documentation integrity and analytics. Changes may affect adoption and billing pathways for AI‑enabled devices/diagnostics.
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers

Regulation Name: Medicare Program: Hospital Outpatient Prospective Payment and ASC Payment Systems; Price Transparency; Radiation Oncology Model
Summary: Finalizes CY 2022 Physician Fee Schedule updates and associated Part B policy changes, including coding/valuation, telehealth, and QPP provisions.
Healthcare Implications: Impacts outpatient care delivery, documentation, and reporting–including AI‑assisted coding and virtual care workflows.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers

Regulation Name: Medicare Program; Hospital Inpatient Prospective Payment Systems and FY 2022 Rates
Summary: Medicare payment policy update for the specified year (IPPS/OPPS), including payment rate recalibrations, quality reporting program changes, and technical corrections.
Healthcare Implications: Expect adjustments to EHR-linked reporting and documentation. AI‑enabled analytics used for quality and revenue integrity should reflect updated specifications.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers; Developers & Vendors

Regulation Name: Medicare Program: Hospital Outpatient Prospective Payment and ASC Payment Systems; Prior Authorization; COVID-19 Reporting and Other Policies
Summary: Finalizes CY 2021 Physician Fee Schedule updates, including E/M coding changes, telehealth expansions, and various Part B policy clarifications.
Healthcare Implications: E/M and telehealth changes drive updates to EHR templates, coding automation, and AI‑assisted documentation/triage tools.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers; Regulators & Government

Regulation Name: Medicare and Medicaid Programs; CY 2021 Physician Fee Schedule and Other Part B Policies
Summary: Companion entry tied to CY 2021 PFS final rule release timing and technical corrections.
Healthcare Implications: Same implications for coding, telehealth, and AI‑assisted outpatient workflows.
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers; Developers & Vendors

Regulation Name: Medicare Program; Revisions to Payment Policies Under the Physician Fee Schedule and Other Part B for CY 2019
Summary: Finalizes CY 2019 updates to the Medicare Physician Fee Schedule (PFS) and other Part B policies, including E/M documentation changes, telehealth-related services, and quality program updates. Adjusts relative value units and addresses coding/valuation changes. Includes technical corrections and clarifications across Part B benefits.
Healthcare Implications: Affects outpatient reimbursement and reporting that drive EHR configuration, coding workflows, and AI‑assisted documentation. Telehealth provisions help define coverage pathways for virtual services supported by AI screening/triage. Clinician groups should align analytics and quality reporting tools with CY 2019 specifications.
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers; Developers & Vendors

Regulation Name: Medicare Program; Revisions to Payment Policies Under the Physician Fee Schedule and Other Part B for CY 2017
Summary: Finalizes CY 2017 changes to the Medicare PFS and related Part B policies, including updates to the Value Modifier and quality programs, coding/valuation adjustments, and other technical changes. Establishes policies foundational to later QPP implementation.
Healthcare Implications: Impacts physician billing and reporting practices that interact with EHR templates and AI‑assisted coding. Quality program changes inform measure capture and analytics pipelines used for performance tracking.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Payers & Purchasers; Developers & Vendors

Regulation Name: Nondiscrimination in Health Programs and Activities
Summary: Implements Section 1557 of the Affordable Care Act for HHS programs, prohibiting discrimination on the basis of race, color, national origin, sex, age, or disability in certain health programs and activities. Establishes requirements for grievance procedures, notices, language access, and reasonable modifications.
Healthcare Implications: Health plans and providers must integrate nondiscrimination practices into operations and digital tools. AI‑mediated triage and decision support should incorporate safeguards against discriminatory outcomes and provide appropriate language access and accommodations.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers

Regulation Name: Medicare Program: Hospital Outpatient Prospective Payment and Ambulatory Surgical Center Payment Systems; Quality Reporting Programs; Price Transparency; Radiation Oncology Model (CY 2021 OPPS/ASC)
Summary: Updates the Medicare hospital Outpatient Prospective Payment System (OPPS) and Ambulatory Surgical Center (ASC) payments for CY 2022; adjusts packaging, device pass‑throughs, and Outpatient Quality Reporting (OQR) measures; and finalizes various policy clarifications.
Healthcare Implications: Influences outpatient coding and reimbursement; hospitals and ASCs should update EHR charge capture and quality dashboards. AI‑assisted documentation integrity and utilization analytics should reflect CY 2022 OPPS/OQR specifications.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers; Developers & Vendors

Regulation Name: Medicare Program: Hospital Outpatient Prospective Payment and Ambulatory Surgical Center Payment Systems (CY 2023 OPPS/ASC)
Summary: Finalizes CY 2024 updates to OPPS and ASC payment systems, revising payment rates, device pass‑through policies, and OQR/ASCQR measures. Includes clarifications and technical changes across outpatient policy areas.
Healthcare Implications: Outpatient reimbursement and reporting updates affect AI‑assisted coding/analytics and quality measure extraction. Hospitals and ASCs should verify measure logic and documentation templates against CY 2024 rules.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers; Developers & Vendors

Regulation Name: Confidentiality of Substance Use Disorder (SUD) Patient Records (42 CFR Part 2)
Summary: Aligns Part 2 confidentiality requirements more closely with HIPAA/HITECH pursuant to the CARES Act, enabling a single patient consent for future uses/disclosures; adds anti‑discrimination protections; harmonizes breach notification and enforcement provisions.
Healthcare Implications: AI analytics and decision support using SUD data must honor Part 2 consent and redisclosure restrictions. Developers and providers should segregate sensitive data and document lawful bases when training or operating models that involve SUD records.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Regulators & Government

Regulation Name: Marketing Submission Recommendations for a Predetermined Change Control Plan (PCCP) for AI/ML-Enabled Device Software Functions
Summary: Final guidance describing how to include a Predetermined Change Control Plan (PCCP) in marketing submissions for AI‑enabled device software functions. Explains PCCP components (Description of Modifications, Modification Protocol, Impact Assessment) and FDA’s review expectations.
Healthcare Implications: Enables post‑market, pre‑authorized model updates without new submissions when within an approved PCCP–accelerating safe iteration of AI‑enabled devices. Hospitals and clinicians can expect timelier performance improvements with clearer labeling and change controls.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems

Regulation Name: Using Artificial Intelligence & Machine Learning in the Development of Drug & Biological Products
Summary: Explores applications of AI/ML across drug and biological product development (e.g., trial design, data integrity, pharmacovigilance), poses questions for stakeholder feedback, and outlines future policy considerations.
Healthcare Implications: Signals regulatory expectations for AI use in clinical trials and manufacturing; sponsors should plan for transparency, validation, and risk management for AI‑enabled methods.

Regulation Name: Artificial Intelligence/Machine Learning (AI/ML)–Based SaMD Action Plan
Summary: Overview of FDA’s approach to AI/ML in Software as a Medical Device (SaMD), including policy initiatives, guiding principles, and links to guidance and resources. Describes regulatory considerations for AI/ML devices and ongoing engagement activities.
Healthcare Implications: Provides implementers and developers with reference materials to align AI/ML device development and post‑market practices with FDA expectations.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public

Regulation Name: Section 1557 Nondiscrimination in Health Programs and Activities
Summary: Finalizes regulations implementing Section 1557 of the ACA, strengthening nondiscrimination protections across covered health programs, including requirements for language access, effective communication, and protections against discrimination in clinical algorithms and telehealth.
Healthcare Implications: Covered entities must assess AI‑mediated tools for discriminatory impacts, ensure accessible digital services, and update policies, training, and notices. Implications span patient intake, triage, benefit design, and algorithm transparency.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Payers & Purchasers; Developers & Vendors

Issuing Body: Office of National Coordinator for Health IT
Regulation Name: HTI-1 Final Rule (Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability)
Summary: Summarizes the ONC HTI‑1 final rule: adopts the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion with transparency for predictive models; adopts USCDI v3; moves to an edition‑less certification framework; updates information blocking; and creates the Insights Condition and Maintenance of Certification.
Healthcare Implications: Impacts EHRs and certified health IT by requiring disclosures for predictive DSIs, improving interoperability, and enabling safer AI integration. Providers and developers must update documentation and compliance practices to meet staged deadlines.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Regulation Name: AI/ML-Based Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) Action Plan
Summary: Outlines FDA’s proposed actions to advance oversight of AI/ML‑based SaMD, including issuing guidances (e.g., PCCP), fostering Good Machine Learning Practice (GMLP), and supporting a total product lifecycle approach.
Healthcare Implications: Signals regulatory roadmap that developers and health systems can use to anticipate requirements for AI device validation, updates, and post‑market monitoring.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Regulators & Government

Regulation Name: Medicare and State Health Care Programs: Fraud and Abuse; Revisions to Safe Harbors and Civil Monetary Penalty Rules
Summary: Revises Anti‑Kickback Statute safe harbors and Civil Monetary Penalty rules to protect certain value‑based arrangements, patient engagement tools, and care coordination supports.
Healthcare Implications: Establishes compliance pathways that can encompass digital health and some AI‑enabled services when structured within protected arrangements, reducing fraud‑and‑abuse risk.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Regulators & Government

Issuing Body: Department of Health and Human Services
Regulation Name: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: Certification Program Updates, Algorithm Transparency, and Information Sharing
Summary: Docket entry with supporting materials and public comments for the ONC HTI‑1 rulemaking.
Healthcare Implications: Provides source materials, public input, and references implementers can consult when operationalizing HTI‑1 requirements (e.g., DSI transparency).
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Regulation Name: Health Breach Notification Rule (HBNR) – Final Rule Amendments
Summary: Amends the HBNR to clarify coverage of health apps and connected devices, expand definitions (e.g., PHR related entities), and specify breach notification triggers and methods, with an effective date of July 29, 2024.
Healthcare Implications: Non‑HIPAA health apps and consumer digital health services handling sensitive data–including AI‑powered apps–must meet stricter breach notification duties. Health systems partnering with such apps should update vendor risk management and incident response plans.
Stakeholder Tags: Developers & Vendors

Regulation Name: NOT-OD-23-149 – Use of Generative AI Technologies is Prohibited for the NIH Peer Review Process
Summary: Clarifies that NIH prohibits peer reviewers from using generative AI or other automated tools to analyze or draft critiques for NIH peer review, citing confidentiality and integrity requirements. Reinforces existing rules under NOT‑OD‑22‑044.
Healthcare Implications: Affects academic medical centers and researchers involved in NIH review. Reinforces data handling and integrity expectations around AI tools in research processes.

Issuing Body: ASTP/Office of the National Coordinator for Health IT
Regulation Name: HTI-2 Final Rule – Trusted Exchange and Information Blocking Updates
Summary: Finalizes select proposals from HTI‑2 to support access, exchange, and use of EHI. Amends information blocking regulations (including TEFCA‑related definitions) and advances Trusted Exchange Framework and Common Agreement (TEFCA) alignment.
Healthcare Implications: Strengthens nationwide interoperability and clarifies information‑blocking scope, enabling safer deployment of AI that depends on cross‑network data access. HIEs, EHRs, and providers should update policies and system behaviors accordingly.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors

Regulation Name: Advisory Committee; Digital Health Advisory Committee; Addition to List of Standing Committees
Summary: Adds the Digital Health Advisory Committee to FDA’s list of standing advisory committees. The committee provides advice on digital health technologies, including AI/ML-enabled devices, remote patient monitoring, clinical decision support, and clinical trial technologies.
Healthcare Implications: Signals ongoing FDA engagement on AI and digital health issues affecting device evaluation and post‑market policies. Clinicians and developers can anticipate structured public input on digital health topics.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Regulation Name: Medical Devices; Gastroenterology-Urology Devices; Classification of the Gastrointestinal Lesion Software Detection System
Summary: Docket record and supporting materials associated with the class II classification of the gastrointestinal lesion software detection system (GI Genius).
Healthcare Implications: Provides background documents and references that clinicians and developers can review for technical and regulatory context regarding AI‑assisted endoscopy tools.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Regulation Name: Artificial Intelligence-Enabled Device Software Functions: Lifecycle Management & Marketing Submission Recommendations
Summary: Draft guidance outlining documentation and lifecycle considerations for marketing submissions involving AI‑enabled device software functions. Provides recommended content for safety/effectiveness evaluation and total product lifecycle (TPLC) practices.
Healthcare Implications: Helps device developers and provider‑innovators structure submissions and ongoing model management for AI‑enabled SaMD/SiMD. Supports iterative improvement with clarity on evidence and transparency expectations.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Regulators & Government

Regulation Name: Considerations for the Use of Artificial Intelligence To Support Regulatory Decision-Making for Drug & Biological Products
Summary: Draft guidance recommending practices for use of AI to generate information intended to support regulatory decisions for drugs/biologics, including transparency, validation, and risk management.
Healthcare Implications: Sponsors using AI in clinical development and CMC should align methods and documentation with FDA expectations, improving credibility and reproducibility of AI‑derived evidence.
Stakeholder Tags: Regulators & Government

Regulation Name: Predetermined Change Control Plans (PCCPs) for Medical Devices
Summary: Announces availability of draft guidance on PCCPs for medical devices, describing how sponsors can pre‑specify certain post‑market modifications and validation methods.
Healthcare Implications: Facilitates responsible iteration of AI‑enabled devices via pre‑authorized change plans, enabling faster updates with appropriate safeguards.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Regulators & Government

Regulation Name: Medical Devices; Laboratory Developed Tests
Summary: Amends FDA regulations to clarify that IVDs, including those manufactured by laboratories, are devices under the FD&C Act; phases out general enforcement discretion and establishes staged compliance timelines over four years.
Healthcare Implications: Clinical laboratories and health systems must plan for registration/listing, quality system, and–where applicable–premarket review. AI‑assisted diagnostics using LDTs should assess impact on validation evidence, labeling, and post‑market obligations.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Regulators & Government

Issuing Body: Note #1: Many of these sector-specific regulations have less direct implications for AI-in-healthcare than in the other sections. I figure that those interested in these niche sector-specific regulations would find this useful.
Note #2: HHS umbrella items -- If an item under “HHS” is clearly HIPAA/ONC/CMS/FDA by its title (e.g., “Information Blocking…”, “HIPAA Privacy Rule…”), keep it Sector-Specific.
